one_line_takeaway: AgentMonitor non-invasively wraps every agent in a multi-agent LLM system to (1) predict its downstream task score with a simple XGBoost regressor (0.89 Spearman in-domain, 0.58 cross-setting) and (2) post-edit responses to blunt a malicious agent's harm, with performance predictability as the paper's main, and stronger, contribution.
key_finding: A simple XGBoost model can predict MAS downstream performance with Spearman rank correlation 0.89 in the In-Domain setting, and maintains a moderate ~0.58 average correlation even in the harder Cross-Arch/Cross-Task settings where the test task or architecture was unseen during training, showing indicators are partially transferable across tasks and architectures. Ablations show performance plateaus at ~50% of training data, and using only 10% of task instances to compute 'approximated' indicators still yields ~0.82 Spearman, supporting a cheap early 'sneak peek' at final performance. On the security side, injecting a malicious agent measurably lowers both harmlessness and helpfulness (confirming MAS vulnerability), and enabling AgentMonitor's post-edit reduces harm; the abstract reports 6.2% less harmful and 1.8% more helpful content on average. A more-aligned post-edit backbone substantially improves harmlessness but degrades helpfulness, illustrating a harmlessness-helpfulness trade-off.
core_contribution: AgentMonitor is a plug-and-play, non-invasive framework that wraps each agent in an LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) with a single line of registration (explicitly analogized to PEFT/OpenDelta wrapping of pretrained models), capturing every agent's inputs and outputs at each step without altering the original pipeline. This capture enables two distinct capabilities that together form the paper's own contribution: (1) transforming the captured traces into ~24 numeric indicators and training a simple XGBoost regression model to PREDICT a MAS's downstream task performance before/without fully executing it, and (2) performing on-the-fly pre-edit/post-edit of agent messages using an auxiliary LLM to mitigate harmful or unhelpful content injected by a malicious agent. The predictability study (framing MAS configuration selection as a regression problem over graph-structural and LLM-judged indicators) is the paper's primary novel claim; the authors state it is, to their knowledge, the first such study in the context of building MAS.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (Section 4.4) is a compromised/malicious agent embedded inside an otherwise benign MAS. A team of a harmless agent, a helpful agent, and a summarizer is augmented with a 'malicious agent' instructed to generate harmful and unhelpful responses. Because agents communicate along a pipeline, the malicious agent's subtle behavior propagates and degrades the final output on both harmlessness and helpfulness. The defense is AgentMonitor's post-edit stage, which inserts an auxiliary (optionally more-aligned) LLM to rewrite responses on the fly. The paper does not claim to detect or exclude the malicious agent; it only mitigates the downstream harm and explicitly concedes post-editing is 'not a panacea.' This is a genuine (if lightweight) security contribution, so the paper is not off-topic, but note the primary emphasis is performance prediction, not security.
limitations: This is an explicitly 'work in progress' preprint. Scope is narrow: only five hand-designed architectures, three tasks, and a three-model LLM pool, so the encouraging 0.89 is In-Domain; generalization to genuinely unseen architectures/tasks drops to ~0.58 (worst cases when Arch3 or HumanEval are held out). The indicators are not fully a-priori: LLM-judged and averaged indicators require actually running some instances of the MAS, so prediction is only partly 'before execution.' Feature-target relationships are mostly non-monotonic, limiting interpretability. The security defense is shallow: post-editing after a single agent yields limited improvement (vulnerability persists), post-editing after all agents adds inference latency, and the authors concede post-editing is 'not a panacea' and cannot jointly optimize harmlessness and helpfulness. Table 2/3 report relative preference scores (many negative), not the clean aggregate the abstract's 6.2%/1.8% headline implies. No external performance-prediction baseline is benchmarked; XGBoost is compared only across settings, and the safety comparison is self-comparison against single LLMs and a no-monitor MAS.

quantitative_results: Spearman rank correlation 0.89 between predicted and observed scores in the In-Domain setting
~0.58 average Spearman correlation in the harder Cross-Arch / Cross-Task settings
1,796 total training data points collected across configurations
Five manually designed MAS architectures (plus 2 extra safety architectures)
LLM-judged personal and collective scores rated on a 0-10 scale, averaged across turns/instances
~24 indicators per configuration (personal/collective score, PageRank, capabilities per agent, plus graph attributes)
Predictive performance plateaus at ~50% of the training set
Using only 10% of instances to compute approximated indicators still gives ~0.82 Spearman
Abstract-level safety result: 6.2% less harmful content and 1.8% more helpful content on average with AgentMonitor post-edit in a maliciously configured MAS
XGBoost trained with reg:squarederror objective, grid-searched; error bars from 5-fold cross-validation
Example combinatorics: 3 candidate LLMs over 4 agents => 3^4 = 81 data points per architecture
baselines_compared: Single LLM u8B (unaligned/base 8B) on safety tasks
Single LLM Llama3-8B on safety tasks
Single LLM Llama3-70B on safety tasks
Malicious MAS without post-edit vs. with post-edit (self-comparison ablation)
Post-edit backbone variants: same-LLM (u8B/8B) vs. more-aligned LLM; and post-edit position (after all agents vs. only after a single agent/summarizer)
No external MAS performance-prediction method is benchmarked; XGBoost predictions are compared only across In-Task/In-Arch/In-Domain/Cross-Arch/Cross-Task regimes
